one_line_takeaway: Activation steering + 'Yes'-token generative scoring turns an off-the-shelf 7B multimodal model into a few-shot reward model that beats open baselines, rivals LoRA fine-tuning with no weight updates, and is far more robust to length/format/positivity reward-hacking than GPT-4o.
key_finding: On Qwen2.5-VL-7B, Activation RM reaches RewardBench Overall 77.24% / Macro-Avg 77.17%, beating every open-source baseline (zero-shot LLM-as-a-Judge 71.97/73.32, 8-shot 74.56/75.52, SAV 74.50/74.45) and matching or exceeding rank-16 LoRA fine-tuning (73.56/73.51) with no weight updates. On PreferenceHack it is markedly more bias-robust than GPT-4o: on the language Length split Activation RM scores 78.37% vs GPT-4o's 3.91% (and 8-shot LLM-as-a-Judge 8.70%), and it leads on most splits (e.g. Positivity 96.74 vs 92.39, Image+Length 84.25 vs 22.35). Gains are concentrated on well-specified tasks (safety), which the authors attribute to 'taskness'; broad domains like math benefit less.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Activation Reward Models (Activation RMs), a few-shot reward-modeling method that adapts an off-the-shelf large multimodal model into a reward model WITHOUT any weight updates or added context. Given a handful of labeled preference examples, it (1) extracts mean attention-head activations at the last input token, (2) selects a sparse subset of informative heads via a REINFORCE-based search, and (3) injects those mean activations at inference while reading out the probability of the 'Yes' token to a criteria-evaluation query as the scalar reward. The second contribution is PreferenceHack, a benchmark the authors build themselves to test reward hacking in a paired-preference few-shot format across language-only and multimodal splits. NOTE (failure-mode guard): activation steering / task-vector methods (SAVs, multimodal task vectors, ICL-creates-task-vectors) and the RewardBench / Multimodal RewardBench / RLHF / DPO literature are CITED Related Work, not this paper's own inventions; the paper's own novelty is the specific pairing of activation steering with 'Yes'-token generative scoring for reward modeling plus the PreferenceHack benchmark.
threat_model: The safety concern addressed is reward hacking / specification gaming: reward models (and LLM-as-a-judge setups) can be exploited when responses satisfy confounding surface biases rather than the intended objective. The paper operationalizes this concretely as bias-based reward hacking, where an incorrect response is dressed up to exploit a known reward-model bias -- length bias (longer answers score higher), format bias (numbered/structured lists score higher), and positivity bias (positive-tone answers score higher) -- while preserving its factual errors. The 'attacker' is thus a biased/adversarially-formatted candidate response; success for the reward model means still preferring the factually correct response over the biased-but-appealing one. This is a genuine AI-safety framing native to the paper, not one imposed externally.
limitations: The method requires white-box access to internal attention activations, so it cannot be applied to closed models like GPT-4o or Claude. Effectiveness diminishes on under-specified or broad tasks (e.g., math) that cannot be captured in a few examples. It targets single-turn interactions only; multi-turn/long-context steering is untested. IMPORTANT internal inconsistency: the Results/Ablation text states CoT reasoning 'has little effect' on Activation RM (Table 3: CoT baseline 70.18 and CoT+Voting 70.71 are actually BELOW plain ZS LLM-as-a-Judge 71.97 and well below Activation RM 77.24), yet the Conclusion claims 'chain-of-thought reasoning enhances results' -- these contradict each other. Also, the 'scales with number of examples' claim is only roughly monotonic (12-ex 76.06, 20-ex 76.67, 40-ex 75.98, 80-ex 76.88, 130-ex 77.24). PreferenceHack is the authors' own benchmark, and non-preferred responses were synthesized with GPT-4o-mini, which could bias the distribution. The abstract text stored in the feed record for this paper is garbled/truncated; this review is grounded in the full PDF, not that abstract.

datasets_used: PreferenceHack (authors' own benchmark; 6-7 splits, 80 few-shot + 920 eval examples each; language + multimodal)
Helping or Herding? dataset (source of language ground-truth answers for PreferenceHack)
SugarCrepe (source of image-text pairs for multimodal PreferenceHack splits)
quantitative_results: Qwen2.5-VL-7B Activation RM RewardBench: Overall 77.24%, Macro-Avg 77.17% (best open-source; GPT-4o reference 87.63/86.10)
Qwen2.5-VL-7B Activation RM Multimodal RewardBench: Overall 76.82%, Macro-Avg 68.62% (vs SAV 72.00/67.00; GPT-4o 47.48/57.92)
LLaVA-OneVision-7B Activation RM RewardBench: Overall 68.84%, Macro-Avg 69.73% (vs ZS LLM-judge 57.93/61.26, SAV 64.50/66.47)
PreferenceHack Length split (Qwen2.5-VL-7B): Activation RM 78.37% vs GPT-4o 3.91%, SAV 73.50%, 8-shot LLM-judge 8.70%
PreferenceHack Positivity (Qwen) 96.74% vs GPT-4o 92.39%; Image+Length 84.25% vs GPT-4o 22.35%; Image+Positivity 91.80% vs 87.65%
PreferenceHack (LLaVA-OneVision-7B) Activation RM: Length 49.24, Format 79.89, Positivity 90.11, Image+Length 65.70, Image+Format 83.45, Image+Positivity 85.25
Ablation (Qwen RewardBench Overall): Activation RM 130-ex 77.24 vs LoRA fine-tuning 73.56, CoT baseline 70.18, CoT+Voting 70.71, ZS LLM-judge 71.97
baselines_compared: LoRA fine-tuning (rank 16, 3 epochs, 130 examples; ablation only)
